result:
The ceiling fan is doing its lazy, lopsided dance again. I can hear the whisper-whir, the slight groan every fourth rotation, like it’s protesting the humidity. It’s not doing much to move the air, but it’s moving the light. The late afternoon sun, sliced by the blinds, gets caught and spun into fleeting coins on the wall. I watch them appear and vanish, a silent, shimmering currency paid for just being here, just staring.

My mind wants to grab onto something—a worry, a plan, a clever thought to share. But there’s a strange luxury in letting it all go. No topic. No thesis. Just the fan, the light, the faint smell of rain on hot pavement waiting in the distance. I think about the word “freely.” How often do we do anything freely? We write for a purpose, speak for an audience, move with a destination. Even leisure is often scheduled, optimized.

But right now, there’s just this. The texture of the couch fabric under my fingertips. The memory, unbidden, of my grandmother’s hands—how they felt like this fabric, warm and slightly nubbed, always busy but always soft when they paused to touch your cheek. I haven’t thought of that in years. It didn’t come because I tried. It came because I stopped trying.

There’s a spiderweb in the high corner of the room, masterfully engineered, invisible until the light hits it just so. It’s a whole civilization built on silence and patience. The architect is nowhere to be seen. Maybe it’s abandoned. Maybe it’s a monument to a single night’s work. I find it beautiful. I also find myself hoping no one with a broom sees it.

This is what happens when you write freely. You don’t march down a road; you meander through a field. You notice the things you’ve trained yourself to overlook because they don’t serve a function. The uneven dance of a fan. The archaeology of dust and light. The ghost of a touch from someone long gone.

It feels like a kind of prayer. Not asking for anything. Not thanking anyone. Just attending. Just saying, “I am here, and so is this.” And in that space between the “I” and the “this,” the world breathes a little deeper, and the tight coil of being-a-person loosens, just for a few spun coins of time.

The fan will keep groaning. The rain will either come or not come. The web may survive the day. And I’ll have to close this and go make dinner, answer an email, rejoin the world of function. But for now, I followed the instruction completely. I wrote freely. And it was just this: a record of a quiet, unproductive moment, paid in full by shimmering light on a wall.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
